questions.question: Explain the six fundamental operations performed on data structures with one example of each.
keyPoints: Traversal: visiting all elements (e.g., printing array elements)
Searching: finding element location (e.g., linear search in unsorted list)
Insertion: adding new element (e.g., adding to end of array)
Deletion: removing element (e.g., removing from linked list)
Sorting: arranging in order (e.g., bubble sort)
Merging: combining sorted structures (e.g., merge two sorted arrays)

questions.question: Derive the time complexity of Binary Search using recurrence relation and Master's Theorem.
keyPoints: Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 for n > 1, T(1) = 1
Applying Master's Theorem: a=1, b=2, f(n)=1
log_b(a) = log_2(1) = 0
Since f(n) = Θ(n^0), case 2 applies
Solution: T(n) = Θ(n^0 log n) = Θ(log n)
Therefore, time complexity is O(log n)

questions.question: Compare the time complexities of insertion operation in arrays versus linked lists.
keyPoints: Array insertion at end: O(1) amortized (if space available), O(n) if resizing needed
Array insertion at beginning: O(n) — must shift all elements
Linked list insertion at beginning: O(1) — just update head pointer
Linked list insertion at end: O(n) to traverse (O(1) with tail pointer)
Space: Arrays have O(1) per element, linked lists have O(n) overhead for pointers

questions.question: What is the difference between stable and unstable sorting algorithms? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Stable sorting algorithms preserve the relative order of equal elements
Unstable algorithms may change the relative order of equal elements
Example of stable: Merge Sort, Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort
Example of unstable: Quick Sort, Heap Sort, Selection Sort
Stability matters when sorting records by multiple keys

questions.question: Analyze the best, average, and worst-case time complexities for Linear Search.
keyPoints: Best Case: Element at first position → O(1), specifically 1 comparison
Average Case: Element equally likely at any position → O(n), specifically (n+1)/2 comparisons
Worst Case: Element at last position or not present → O(n), specifically n comparisons
Assumptions: Element is present with probability p, uniformly distributed

questions.question: Explain the Merge operation in detail with an algorithm. Calculate the time complexity by analyzing the merge process step-by-step.
keyPoints: Merge operation combines two sorted sequences into one sorted sequence
Algorithm: Use three pointers — i for first array, j for second array, k for result
Initialize i=j=0, compare elements at both pointers, copy smaller to result
Continue until one array exhausted, then copy remaining elements
Time complexity: Each element processed exactly once → O(m+n) where m,n are array sizes
Space complexity: O(m+n) for auxiliary array, or O(1) if merging in-place
Proof: At most (m+n-1) comparisons, each comparison advances one pointer

questions.question: Derive the time complexity of Merge Sort using recurrence relation. Show how Master's Theorem gives O(n log n). Explain why this is optimal for comparison-based sorting.
keyPoints: Merge Sort recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) for n > 1, T(1) = O(1)
Division: O(1) to find midpoint
Recursion: Sort two halves — 2T(n/2)
Merge: O(n) to combine sorted halves
Applying Master's Theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n
Since log_b(a) = log_2(2) = 1, and f(n) = Θ(n^1)
Case 1 applies: T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a)) = Θ(n^1) = O(n log n)
Optimality: Comparison-based sorting requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in worst case
This lower bound is proven by decision tree model

questions.question: Compare and contrast Linear Search and Binary Search with respect to their time complexities, prerequisites, and practical applications.
keyPoints: Linear Search: O(n) time, works on sorted/unsorted arrays, O(1) space
Binary Search: O(log n) time, requires sorted array, O(1) iterative / O(log n) recursive space
Binary Search prerequisite: Data must be sorted (or sortable) and allow random access
For n=10^6: Linear worst-case = 10^6 comparisons, Binary worst-case = 20 comparisons
Practical applications: Linear for small/n sorted data, Binary for large sorted datasets
Trade-offs: Binary requires sorted data and random access (arrays), Linear works on linked lists
Hybrid approaches: Interpolated search for uniformly distributed data
